head: 44c1476
depth_rationale: 2603-line diff touching cache + failsafe data-mutation hot paths + request routing; user explicitly flagged auth/payment-class risks (data-mutation, hot-path).

findings.title: Cascade: partial user failsafe silently strips timeout/retry from upstreamDefaults, leaving requests with zero protection
evidence: Trigger config: `upstreamDefaults.failsafe = [{matchMethod: '*', timeout: 30s, retry: {maxAttempts: 3}}]` and a per-upstream override `failsafe: [{matchers: [{method: '*', action: include}], retry: {maxAttempts: 5}}]` (no timeout block).

Path: `UpstreamConfig.SetDefaults` line 1673 takes the `len(u.Failsafe) > 0` branch and calls `fs.SetDefaults(nil)` for every user config (line 1679 — the `nil` is intentional per the in-source comment 'matchers PR (#388) deliberately replaced per-method/finality merging with user policies override defaults entirely').

FailsafeConfig.SetDefaults(nil) (defaults.go:2075) only fills in zero-valued fields *within the user's own config*. Since `f.Timeout == nil`, the branch `else if defaults != nil && defaults.Timeout != nil` (line 2088) is skipped because defaults is nil. Result: `f.Timeout` stays nil; no timeout context is ever applied in `Network.Forward` line 499 (`if failsafeExecutor.timeout != nil`).

Same pattern repeats verbatim in `NetworkConfig.SetDefaults` (defaults.go:1810-1828) for network-level failsafe — a user defining only retry at the network level loses the network-level timeout from networkDefaults.

Outcome: at runtime, `Network.Forward` line 499 sees nil timeout function, never wraps the request context. A retrying eth_call against a stuck upstream now blocks for the full server.maxTimeout (default 150s, per defaults.go:746) instead of the user-intended 30s. Under hedge or retry pressure during an incident, the network forwarder can hold thousands of goroutines at the maxTimeout ceiling — exactly the failure mode failsafe was meant to prevent.
findings.scenario: Production deployment migrates configs by adding upstreamDefaults at the top level (timeout+retry) and keeps existing per-upstream failsafe overrides that only override retry. Pre-PR each upstream got the merged 30s timeout; post-PR it gets nil → unbounded latency tail under upstream slowness.
findings.matches_existing_concern: Aram review threads #11/#12 (BLOCKER per PR author)
findings.fix_direction: Either (a) restore per-policy merging when defaults exist for the same matcher, or (b) refuse to load a config where user's failsafe omits a sub-policy that defaults provide and emit a validation error so the override is explicit.

findings.title: Assumption violation: convertLegacyMatchers loses finality and empty constraints when method/network/params are unset
evidence: Line 622: `hasLegacyFields := c.Network != "" || c.Method != "" || len(c.Params) > 0` — this expression deliberately omits `c.Finality != DataFinalityStateUnknown` and `c.Empty != CacheEmptyBehaviorIgnore` (the zero values).
Trigger config: `policies: [{connector: 'redis-1', finality: 'finalized', empty: 'ignore', ttl: '1h'}]` (no method/network/params).

Path: `hasLegacyFields == false` so the if-block at line 626 is skipped. The fallback at line 660 fires: `c.Matchers = append(c.Matchers, &MatcherConfig{Method: '*', Action: MatcherInclude})`. The synthetic catch-all has zero-valued Finality (empty slice) and zero-valued Empty.

Outcome: the user explicitly said 'cache only finalized data, ignore empty results'; the policy actually caches every method including unfinalized and emptyish responses on every connector match. Cached unfinalized data may be served as 'final answer' for hours — exactly the data-correctness hazard the user opted into the constraints to avoid.

Compounding factor: zero value of `CacheEmptyBehavior` is `CacheEmptyBehaviorIgnore (iota=0)` per common/data.go:69-75, so the auto-catch-all matcher inherits the 'don't cache empty' semantic but the user's explicit `empty: ignore` was already that — the symmetry hides the loss for *empty* but the *finalized* loss is observable and dangerous.
findings.scenario: User migrates from legacy schema where they only specified `finality: finalized, empty: ignore` (no method/network), assumes the conversion preserves the constraint, and caches reorgable unfinalized data into a 1h-TTL Redis policy. After a chain reorg, eRPC serves stale unfinalized data for up to an hour.
findings.fix_direction: Detect explicit Finality and Empty by also checking `c.Finality != DataFinalityStateUnknown` (or use a *DataFinalityState pointer to distinguish unset from unknown), and surface a validation error when finality-only configs are present so users either upgrade to matchers or get an explicit warning.

findings.title: Composition failure: SET/GET matcher asymmetry for exclude+finality renders successfully cached data permanently unreadable
evidence: SET path (cache_policy.go:99-121 → matchers.MatchConfig): on a finalized eth_getBalance, iteration is reverse (last first). Matcher[1] (`finality: [unfinalized]`) calls MatchConfig which requires *strict* finality match (matcher.go:38-49 — finalized request, matcher slice contains only Unfinalized → no match). Iteration continues. Matcher[0] matches → cache write succeeds. Data lands in Redis.

GET path (cache_policy.go:129-204): same iteration order, but the GET-side has its own divergent finality logic (line 175-183): `if finality == DataFinalityStateFinalized { for _, f := range matcher.Finality { if f == Finalized || f == Unfinalized { finalityMatches = true } } }`. For the same finalized request: matcher[1] finalityMatches=true (because `f == Unfinalized` triggers via the 'finalized requests can match unfinalized policies' clause). Action=exclude → return `false, nil` immediately at line 199.

Outcome: SET stored the eth_getBalance result; GET refuses to look it up because the wildcard exclude matcher's finality flexibility falsely matches finalized requests too. The cache entry persists for the full TTL (potentially hours/days) consuming Redis memory and forcing every read to bypass cache.

Note: the GET-side flexibility is documented (line 175-176) as a feature for 'data cached as unfinalized but is now finalized', but it was not adapted to the inverse case where an exclude matcher targets unfinalized — the flexibility hijacks the matcher's intent.
findings.scenario: Production user adds an exclude matcher to skip caching of unfinalized data. After the next deploy, cache hit rate drops to ~0% for eth_getBalance even though SETs succeed; metrics show cache_set_success rising while cache_get_hit stays flat. Postmortem traces back to the GET-side finalized→unfinalized flexibility silently matching the wildcard exclude.
findings.fix_direction: Either (a) make GET use the same MatchConfig as SET so iteration is symmetric, OR (b) only apply the finalized→unfinalized flexibility when matcher.Action == include, never when action == exclude. Verify with a regression test that mirrors the trigger config above.

findings.title: Abuse case: validateMatchers' auto-prepended catch-all-exclude silently strips failsafe protection from non-matched methods
evidence:
Trigger config (intuitive intent: 'apply this failsafe to eth_* methods'):
```yaml
failsafe:
  - matchers: [{method: 'eth_*', action: include}]
    timeout: 1s
    retry: {maxAttempts: 5}
```
validation.go:54-61 sees only-include actions and prepends a catch-all-exclude: matchers becomes `[{method: '*', action: exclude}, {method: 'eth_*', action: include}]`.

Runtime path (networks.go:259-272): for a request to `trace_block`: matchers.Match iterates last-to-first. Matcher[1] (eth_*) doesn't match. Matcher[0] (*, exclude) matches → returns `Action == MatcherInclude` → false. getFailsafeExecutor `continue`s (line 271).

After the loop, line 287-289 falls back to the LAST registered executor. But the auto-default catch-all appended at networks_registry.go:148-156 (and upstream.go:110-116) has `config: nil, timeout: nil, executor: failsafe.NewExecutor[*common.NormalizedResponse]()` — i.e. no timeout, no retry, no hedge.

Outcome: trace_block requests have ZERO failsafe protection. A misbehaving upstream serving slow trace responses can hang Network.Forward calls for the full server.maxTimeout (150s default) without retry budgets, hedge, or circuit-breaker — unlike eth_* which has 1s timeout + 5 retries.

Why the user can't see this from config-validation alone: `validateMatchers` SUCCEEDS with the user's input. The fallback to no-protection is silent runtime behavior produced by the auto-prepended catch-all-exclude + auto-appended catch-all empty-executor interacting through getFailsafeExecutor's iteration.
findings.scenario: Operator scopes failsafe to `eth_*` thinking 'other methods will use defaults'. After deploying, they observe trace_block and debug_traceTransaction requests pile up during upstream slowness because no timeout fires. Aggravated by the user-rules requirement that the legacy schema's MatchMethod='*' default produced full coverage — they don't realize switching to matchers semantically changes the fallback.
findings.fix_direction: Either (a) when an executor has matchers and no catch-all include exists, refuse to register the executor without surfacing a validation warning, OR (b) auto-append a clone of the user's last failsafe (without matchers) as the catch-all instead of an empty executor, OR (c) document and enforce that matcher-based failsafe configs must end with an explicit `{method: '*', action: include}` matcher and fail validation otherwise.

findings.title: Composition failure: MatchesForGetWithMatcher hand-rolls matching logic that drifts from MatchConfig — Empty constraint silently ignored at GET
evidence: MatchesForSetWithMatcher (cache_policy.go:99-121) delegates to `matchers.MatchConfig` for its match decision. MatchesForGetWithMatcher (line 129-204) duplicates a divergent subset of the logic inline: it checks Network, Method, Params, and Finality but skips the `config.Empty` check entirely (compare to matcher.go:52-57).

This means: a user policy `{method: 'eth_call', empty: 'only', action: include}` will match a GET for any non-empty eth_call. The empty-only intent is enforced at SET-time (cache only empty responses) but at GET-time the policy is checked for ANY result type. With multiple policies to multiple connectors, dedup at line 791-797 picks the first matching one — potentially the empty-only policy — and dispatches to its connector for a request whose result the policy was never meant to cover.

Drift risk: any future change to MatchConfig (e.g., adding a network-architecture filter, or changing wildcard semantics) will be silently absent from MatchesForGetWithMatcher, producing SET/GET mismatch on the new field too.

The post-Get switch at json_rpc_cache.go:336-362 treats the cached jrr's emptiness against `policyWithMatcher.EmptyState()` — which IS the matcher's Empty field — but only AFTER a connector lookup has already occurred. The mis-routing to the wrong connector still wastes a network round-trip on every miss.
findings.scenario: A user defines two cache policies on different connectors: (P1) `{method: '*', empty: 'only', connector: 'redis-empties'}` for cheap empty-result caching, and (P2) `{method: '*', empty: 'allow', connector: 'redis-rich'}`. SET writes empties to P1 and non-empties to P2 correctly. But GET sees both policies match (Empty ignored), dedup picks the first by config order. If P1 is first, every eth_call lookup queries redis-empties first, gets a miss for any non-empty key, and only then would fall through — but the loop breaks on the first non-error return so the second connector is never queried for non-empties. Cache hit rate collapses for non-empty results.
findings.fix_direction: Replace the inline matching in MatchesForGetWithMatcher with a call to a shared MatchConfig variant that accepts a 'flexible finality' flag, so the dedup path stays consistent with SET.

findings.title: Performance/composition: per-request matcher evaluation tokenizes wildcards from scratch on every JSON-RPC call
evidence: matchers.Match runs for every request at two hot spots: getFailsafeExecutor (networks.go:483 + upstream.go:604) and findGet/SetPolicies (json_rpc_cache.go:749/774).

common/matcher.go:34-47: `WildcardMatch` allocates fresh tokens, then a fresh parser, then a closure-based matcher AST per call — no caching, no precompiled state. tokenize() and parseExpression() are invoked on every Network/Method comparison.

Per request work at 10k rps with N executors × M matchers × P param entries: e.g., 3 executors × 3 matchers × 5 params + 5 cache policies × 3 matchers × 5 params = 120 WildcardMatch invocations × 10000 rps = 1.2M tokenize+parse calls/second. Each call allocates Go slices/closures that GC must reclaim.

matchers.Match has no internal cache (no MatchResultCache; the user-attached concern about 'race conditions in matcher cache' is a non-issue because the cache simply doesn't exist). All compounding optimization opportunity is missing.
MatchParams (matcher.go:63-82) recurses through map/array params on every call: for a structured eth_getLogs filter object, every key-value pair triggers another WildcardMatch + tokenize cycle.
findings.scenario: Production replays at 8-10k rps (per Aram #18 BLOCKER): tokenize allocations dominate gctimes, p99 latency spikes during high-cardinality method workloads (mixed eth_*, debug_*, trace_* with structured filter params). Effect compounds when cache policies and failsafe executors both pattern-match on every request. The PR ships without micro-benchmark comparing against the pre-PR per-attribute checks.
findings.fix_direction: Pre-tokenize patterns once at config load (cache the parsed matcher AST in MatcherConfig as an unexported field). Optionally add a per-request match-result cache keyed by (networkId, method, finality, params hash). Ship the rps benchmark Aram requested before merging.

findings.title: Assumption violation: zero-value of MatcherConfig.Empty is CacheEmptyBehaviorIgnore, silently disabling empty-response caching on synthetic catch-all matchers
evidence: common/data.go:69-75 declares `type CacheEmptyBehavior int` with `CacheEmptyBehaviorIgnore = iota` at value 0. The zero value of any uninitialized matcher.Empty is therefore `Ignore`.

convertLegacyMatchers (defaults.go:660-665) and FailsafeConfig.Normalize (defaults.go:2067-2073) both append a synthetic catch-all `&MatcherConfig{Method: '*', Action: MatcherInclude}` without setting Empty. The Empty field defaults to Ignore.
matcher.go:52-53 then evaluates `if isEmptyish && config.Empty == CacheEmptyBehaviorIgnore { return false }` — the synthetic catch-all silently rejects emptyish responses.

For cache SET path: a user who configured nothing more than `{connector: 'redis', ttl: '1h'}` thinks they have a permissive cache-everything policy. They actually have a policy that excludes empty responses (`null`, `[]`, `0x`). Methods like eth_getLogs returning `[]` are not cached, causing repeated upstream calls for the empty-result hot case.
For failsafe Match() path: harmless because resp is nil so isEmptyish is always false. So the bug is scoped to cache policy matching.
Compounds with adv-002: a user trying to migrate from `empty: allow` legacy semantics to matcher-style with no Empty field set will silently flip behavior.
findings.scenario: User upgrades to the matchers PR with their legacy config that omitted `empty:` (relying on prior default behavior). Empty results that previously hit cache now miss every time, doubling load on upstreams for the empty-result hot path until the user notices in metrics.
findings.fix_direction: Either (a) change the iota ordering so `CacheEmptyBehaviorAllow = iota` (0 = allow = no constraint) and migrate any existing serializations, OR (b) explicitly set `Empty: CacheEmptyBehaviorAllow` on every synthetic catch-all matcher constructor, OR (c) change the type to a pointer/string so unset is detectable. A migration changelog entry is essential either way.

residual_risks: Order-dependence under config hot reload: if `c.policies` or `failsafeExecutors` are ever swapped out at runtime (admin endpoint, config refresh), the slice header read in matchers.Match is unsynchronized — racy under load. Could not find evidence of runtime swap in this PR but the design surface invites it.

Race window in matchers.Match's `params = jrpcReq.Params` (matchers/matcher.go:166) read without the JsonRpcRequest's RLock. Currently safe because Match runs before request normalization mutates Params (see networks.go:438 prepareRequest is post-getFailsafeExecutor for failsafe selection — wait, prepareRequest runs at line 438 BEFORE failsafe at 483, so params have already been mutated atomically; further per-attempt eth_call.go mutations at upstream.go runtime occur INSIDE the failsafe executor — after Match returned). Brittle: any future caller invoking matchers.Match during in-flight upstream execution would race with eth_call.go:23's `jrq.Lock(); jrq.Params = ...`.

Aram #16 cache_policy SetDefaults shim risk: NewCachePolicy now mutates the passed CachePolicyConfig via SetDefaults, so callers reusing a config pointer across NewCachePolicy invocations get accumulated state. Not a problem within EvmJsonRpcCache.NewEvmJsonRpcCache but library consumers integrating data.NewCachePolicy directly may be surprised.

Pre-existing `findGetPolicies` connector-dedup (json_rpc_cache.go:776-797) interacts with the new age-guard (shouldAcceptCachedResult) to short-circuit fallback policies on the same connector. If the first matching policy's age guard rejects, all subsequent policies pointing to the same connector are skipped — a user defining a 5s realtime + 1h fallback on Redis loses the fallback path. NOT introduced by this PR but the new age-guard makes the failure mode reachable without TTL expiry.
testing_gaps: No integration test exercises the partial-failsafe-override-with-defaults case (adv-001). Tests should construct a project with upstreamDefaults.failsafe = [timeout, retry] and a per-upstream override with only retry, then assert that a request against a slow upstream times out at the defaults value (not at server.maxTimeout).

No table-driven test for convertLegacyMatchers covering finality-only / empty-only legacy configs (adv-002). Add cases: `{finality: finalized}`, `{empty: ignore}`, `{finality: realtime, empty: only}` with no method/network/params, asserting that the resulting matcher preserves the constraint.

No test demonstrates SET/GET symmetry under exclude matchers with finality (adv-003). Construct config, call MatchesForSet then MatchesForGet with the same (network, method, params, finality), assert equal match results when Action=Exclude is involved.
No coverage for failsafe matchers + non-eth methods (adv-004): assert that a config with only `eth_*` matchers does NOT silently downgrade trace_* and debug_* methods to the empty fallback executor.

No production-realistic rps benchmark (Aram #18 BLOCKER, adv-006). Need to run mixed-method (eth_*, trace_*, debug_*) at 8-10k rps with hedge enabled and compare allocation rate / p99 latency against pre-PR baseline.

No fuzz test on validateMatchers / convertLegacyMatchers — both are deterministic transformations producing user-visible behavior, ideal for property-based testing (e.g., 'every legacy config converts losslessly to a matcher that produces equivalent decisions for all inputs').
